Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd try to set up times to study, but then something would come up—work shifts, family stuff, just life being chaotic. Most weeks I'd end up cramming everything into one or two late nights when I couldn't avoid it anymore. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so sitting and reading online materials felt really unnatural to me. I'd often just wing it and hope something stuck.
Q2: My pattern was completely dependent on deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. There was no regular schedule—I'd go days without touching anything, then panic when I realized something was due. Sometimes my extracurricular commitments would eat up all my time, and studying just got pushed to the back burner. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but it never really happened.
Q3: Usually panic. Like, I'd get an email reminder that something was due in 24 hours and I'd suddenly log in freaking out. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking if I'd done something and I'd realize I hadn't even looked at it. The peer support thing they set up helped a bit—my partner would sometimes check in and that would prompt me to log in. But honestly, it was rarely a proactive choice.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every few days. But then I missed a few days, then a week, and it just fell apart. The LMS never felt natural to me—it's all clicking and reading, which isn't how I learn best. I need to physically do things to understand them, and the platform didn't offer that.
Q5: The videos were actually helpful when I could focus on them. I watched a lot of them—like, hours worth—because I could at least see someone demonstrating concepts. That felt closer to hands-on learning than just reading text. The few times I posted in discussions and got responses, that helped too because it made the material feel more real. But most resources felt pretty disconnected from how I actually learn.
Q6: I ignored most of the reading materials and the discussion boards after a while. The readings were just walls of text and I'd zone out after a few paragraphs. With my anxiety being what it is, I'd open something, feel overwhelmed, and just close it. The optional stuff? Forget it. I barely had energy for the required things. I kept telling myself I'd come back to them but never did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something practical. Like, if I could apply it to something real or if completing it meant I was done with a requirement. The problem was most things felt abstract and I couldn't see the point. When I'm already stressed and anxious, I need to feel like my effort is going somewhere concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're already drowning trying to keep up with the required stuff, optional feels like a joke. Required things had this weight of consequence—fail this, and you're in trouble. Optional things just felt like more noise when I was already overwhelmed. I think I did maybe one optional thing the entire course.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download videos and PDFs to my phone and try to study during bus rides or breaks at work. With limited resources, I couldn't always be online, so offline studying was essential. I'd also make physical notes—writing things out by hand helps me remember better than typing. Sometimes I'd practice problems on paper that never showed up in any system.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours going through downloaded materials, making handwritten notes, trying to work through problems physically. None of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with my peer support partner in person or over text, working things out verbally. The LMS only saw me logging in briefly, but the real studying was happening offline, away from any tracking.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then study from it for days without going back online. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab everything I needed in one session. The LMS probably showed me accessing something for five minutes, but I'd spend hours with it later. It was just more practical for my situation.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart. My anxiety spiked really hard—I was having trouble sleeping, couldn't focus, felt like I was failing at everything. Work got more demanding, and I had family stuff going on that I can't really get into. The course material started piling up and I just... froze. I'd log in, see everything I'd missed, and feel so overwhelmed I'd log right back out.
Q13: Massively. Fatigue was constant—between work, extracurriculars, and trying to study, I was running on empty. When I was exhausted, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening because it just reminded me of everything I was behind on. Confusion about assignments made it worse; I'd spend time trying to figure out what I was supposed to do instead of actually doing it. Competing responsibilities meant the course always lost.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about money problems or whatever was stressing me out, and the material just wouldn't register. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. It looked like engagement but it was just... presence without learning.
Q15: Constantly. The exam studying especially—I knew I needed to prepare but I kept putting it off because the anxiety around it was paralyzing. The thought of facing how behind I was made me avoid it more. It's this terrible cycle where avoiding something makes the anxiety worse, which makes you avoid it more. I knew it was important but knowing doesn't always translate to doing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore stuff until it was almost due, then scramble. The LMS would show these bursts of activity right before deadlines and nothing in between. It's not a healthy way to work, and it meant I was always stressed and never really learning deeply. But without that pressure, I just couldn't make myself engage.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was a mixed bag. Positive feedback gave me a tiny boost, made me feel like maybe I could do this. But critical feedback, even constructive stuff, would send my anxiety through the roof. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was scared of what I'd see. The feedback loop was broken because I was too anxious to engage with it properly.
Q18: The structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for me. Everything was on a set schedule that didn't account for life happening. I needed more flexibility, more hands-on stuff, more ways to catch up when I fell behind. The linear progression meant once you missed something, you felt perpetually behind. It made it hard to feel like I could ever get back on track.
Q19: The lack of hands-on, practical activities made it really hard for me. I'm a kinesthetic learner—I need to do things, not just read about them. The course was very text and video heavy, which isn't how I process information best. Also, the way everything was spread across different sections made it confusing to navigate when I was already stressed and short on time.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, honestly. You're logging in, clicking around, trying to figure things out, watching videos, but you're so overwhelmed or confused that actually completing and submitting work feels impossible. Or maybe they're engaging with the content but struggling to produce something that meets the requirements. Activity doesn't equal output when you're drowning.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of me, but I can see it. Some people are just efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it, and do the work offline. Or they have other resources, like textbooks or study groups, that don't show up in the LMS. The system only sees one slice of how someone learns. Someone could be doing great work that just doesn't happen on the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They can't see that I downloaded something and studied it for hours offline. They can't measure anxiety or how hard it was just to open the platform some days. They show activity but miss the human stuff—the struggle, the offline work, the real learning that happens away from screens.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning. It's not just going through the motions—it's when the material clicks and you want to understand more. For me, engagement happens when I can do something with my hands, apply concepts practically, feel like it matters. This course... I wanted to be engaged, but everything worked against that. The engagement was there in bursts, but not sustained.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before logging in, the hours of offline studying, the conversations with my peer support partner that actually helped me understand things. It wouldn't show that I was working a job and dealing with limited resources while trying to keep up. It just shows a failing student, not the full picture of someone in crisis trying their best.
